tldr: Xata is a general-purpose serverless Postgres; QuestDB is a time-series database with a SQL surface and ingestion-optimized storage. This migration only makes sense for time-series workloads inside your Xata data, and it is not a one-to-one move: schema, query patterns, and indexing strategy all need to be rethought.
why_switch: Optimize storage and queries for high-throughput time-series data
Use QuestDB's ingestion path for fast appends and downsampling
Run window and time-bucket queries with native SQL extensions
Self-host or use managed QuestDB for cost control
Reduce general-purpose database load by isolating metrics/events
what_to_export: Timestamp column and any required partition keys
Schema definitions for the tables you are migrating
Indexes that map to QuestDB symbol columns
Retention or archival rules in effect
Row counts and time ranges per table
export_method: Identify only the tables that are genuinely time-series; leave transactional tables on Postgres. From Xata, get a Postgres connection string and export each candidate table ordered by timestamp: COPY (SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY ts) TO STDOUT WITH CSV HEADER > events.csv. For large tables, chunk by time range to keep file sizes manageable and to allow partial re-runs. Capture the DDL separately so you can translate it. Maintain a manifest of files with row counts, min/max timestamps, and checksums. If continuous writes happen during export, plan a cutover with a short read-only window or stream new rows separately and merge after initial load. Verify CSV integrity (no embedded newlines without quoting) before loading.
import_method: Create a QuestDB instance (managed cloud or self-hosted). Translate the source DDL: pick a designated timestamp column, choose PARTITION BY (DAY/MONTH) sized to your retention, and use SYMBOL types for low-cardinality string dimensions instead of TEXT. Create tables with CREATE TABLE ... TIMESTAMP(ts) PARTITION BY DAY. Load data with the recommended path for your volume: small datasets via the REST imp endpoint or web console CSV import; larger datasets via the InfluxDB Line Protocol over TCP/HTTP for streaming-style ingestion. Validate by comparing row counts and time-range aggregates between source and QuestDB. Update applications to use QuestDB drivers (Postgres-wire SQL is supported for queries; ILP for writes). Rewrite analytical queries to use SAMPLE BY, LATEST ON, and ASOF JOIN where appropriate. Keep the original Xata data read-only until QuestDB is verified.

concept_mapping.note: Time pruning is automatic by partition

concept_mapping.target: SAMPLE BY query or downsampled table

concept_mapping.note: QuestDB is optimized for time-series, not heavy relational joins

gotchas: QuestDB is not a drop-in Postgres replacement; only migrate time-series data.
Choosing the wrong PARTITION BY granularity hurts ingest and query performance.
Large TEXT cardinality as SYMBOL leads to memory pressure; profile before committing.
Transactions and constraints behave differently from Postgres; rewrite write paths accordingly.
Tooling and ORM support is narrower; expect to write more raw SQL.
Backups and HA models differ from a managed Postgres service; design the operational story up front.

email.subject: Time-series workloads moving from Xata to QuestDB in 14 days
email.body:
Hi,

We are migrating our time-series data (metrics and event streams) from Xata to QuestDB over the next 14 days. The rest of the application database is unaffected.

What to expect:
- No change to product features.
- A short read-only window on the time-series tables during final sync; the exact time will be announced 48 hours in advance.
- Faster dashboards and analytical queries once QuestDB is live.

What does not change:
- Your account, transactional data, and integrations.
- Pricing and support terms.

If your team queries our metrics tables directly, reply so we can share the new connection details and updated query patterns where they differ.

Thanks for your patience as we tune the platform for scale.

Your customer success team
